section.title: Abraham Justified by Faith
section.sectionRationale: Having declared in chapter 3 that God justifies sinners freely by grace through faith apart from works of the law, Paul now proves it from the Scriptures using the towering example of Abraham. If anyone could have boasted before God, it was the father of the Jewish nation; yet Genesis says Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness. Paul shows that this crediting came by faith and not by works, before circumcision and before the law, so that Abraham is the father of all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike. The chapter climbs to a full portrait of saving faith: against all hope Abraham believed in hope, fully convinced that God could do what He had promised, and closes by turning that same faith toward us who trust the God who raised Jesus.
section.thesis: Abraham was justified not by works but by faith, credited as righteousness before he was circumcised and apart from the law, so that he is the father of all who believe; and the same righteousness is credited to all who trust in the God who raised Jesus, who was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Abraham credited righteous by faith and not by works; the blessing given before circumcision so that he fathers Jew and Gentile; the promise received through faith and not law; and the portrait of Abraham's faith against hope, applied to us who believe in Him who raised Jesus.

units.label: Credited by faith, not works
units.note: Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him

verses.text: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, has discovered?
If Abraham was indeed justified by works, he had something to boast about, but not before God.
For what does the Scripture say? “Abraham believed God, and it was {{logizomai|credited}} to him as {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}}.”
Now the wages of the worker are not credited as a gift, but as an obligation.
However, to the one who does not work, but believes in Him who justifies the ungodly, his {{pistis|faith}} is credited as righteousness.
And David speaks likewise of the blessedness of the man to whom God credits righteousness apart from works:
“Blessed are they whose lawless acts are forgiven, whose sins are covered.
Blessed is the man whose sin the Lord will never count against him.”
Is this blessing only on the circumcised, or also on the uncircumcised? We have been saying that Abraham’s faith was credited to him as righteousness.
In what context was it credited? Was it after his circumcision, or before? It was not after, but before.

And he received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness that he had by faith while he was still uncircumcised. So then, he is the father of all who believe but are not circumcised, in order that righteousness might be credited to them.
And he is also the father of the circumcised who not only are circumcised, but who also walk in the footsteps of the faith that our father Abraham had before he was circumcised.

units.label: The promise through faith, not law
units.note: so that it may rest on grace

verses.text: For the promise to Abraham and his offspring that he would be heir of the world was not given through the law, but through the righteousness that comes by faith.
For if those who live by the law are heirs, faith is useless and the promise is worthless,
because the law brings wrath. And where there is no law, there is no transgression.

Therefore, the promise comes by faith, so that it may rest on grace and may be guaranteed to all Abraham’s offspring, not only to those who are of the law, but also to those who are of the faith of Abraham. He is the father of us all.

As it is written: “I have made you a father of many nations.” He is our father in the presence of God, in whom he believed, the God who gives life to the dead and calls into being what does not yet exist.

units.label: Against hope, believing in hope
units.note: fully persuaded God could do what He promised

verses.text: Against all hope, Abraham in {{elpis|hope}} believed and so became the father of many nations, just as he had been told, “So shall your offspring be.”
Without weakening in his faith, he acknowledged the decrepitness of his body (since he was about a hundred years old) and the lifelessness of Sarah’s womb.
Yet he did not waver through disbelief in the promise of God, but was strengthened in his faith and gave glory to God,
being fully persuaded that God was able to do what He had promised.
This is why “it was credited to him as righteousness.”
Now the words “it was credited to him” were written not only for Abraham,
but also for us, to whom righteousness will be credited, for us who believe in Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead.
He was delivered over to death for our trespasses and was raised to life for our justification.

logizomai.gloss: To reckon, count, credit, or put to one's account. It is an accounting and legal word, used eleven times in this chapter, describing how God enters righteousness on the ledger of the one who believes. Crucially, the righteousness credited is not the believer's own achievement but a gift reckoned to him; God counts the ungodly righteous on the ground of faith, not works, exactly as Genesis 15:6 says of Abraham.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, the state of being right with God, of standing acquitted before His court. In Romans this righteousness is God's own gift, revealed in the gospel and received by faith. Abraham's righteousness was not earned by his works but credited to him when he believed, so that the standing he had before God came from outside himself, from God's reckoning.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. Justifying faith is not a work that earns a wage but the empty hand that receives what God freely gives. Paul sets faith and works in sharp contrast: the worker is paid an obligation, but the believer, who does not work but trusts Him who justifies the ungodly, has faith itself credited as righteousness. Faith looks away from self to the promising God.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation grounded in God's promise. Abraham's faith is shown as hope against hope: with his own body as good as dead and Sarah's womb barren, he had no natural reason to expect a son, yet he hoped in the God who gives life to the dead. Biblical hope is not wishful thinking but settled assurance resting on the character and word of God.

basic.context: Paul has just argued that God justifies sinners freely by grace through faith, apart from works of the law, so that no one can boast. Now he anticipates the obvious objection: what about Abraham, the father of the Jewish people? Surely he was accepted for his obedience. Paul answers by going to the Scripture itself. Genesis says Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness. That single sentence proves justification is by faith, not works, and it was written for our sake as much as his.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter is a sustained exposition of one verse, Genesis 15:6, backed by Psalm 32. Read it as a courtroom and accounting argument. The key word credited, or reckoned, appears again and again. Paul reasons about timing (before circumcision), about basis (faith not law), and about scope (Jew and Gentile), and then paints faith itself in the portrait of Abraham. The whole chapter aims to show that the way Abraham was justified is exactly the way we are.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on logizomai, to credit or reckon, an accounting term for entering righteousness on a person's account. That righteousness is dikaiosyne, a right standing before God, given not earned. It is received by pistis, faith, the trust that does not work for a wage but receives a gift. And Abraham's faith is elpis, hope against hope, confident that the life-giving God would keep His word.
basic.typology: Abraham is more than an example; he is the pattern father of a worldwide family of faith. The promise that he would be father of many nations reaches its fulfillment as Gentiles come to share his faith. The God who brought life from Abraham's dead body and Sarah's barren womb is the same God who raised Jesus from the dead, so Abraham's story foreshadows the resurrection at the heart of the gospel.
basic.christ: The chapter ends at the cross and the empty tomb. The same faith that justified Abraham now rests on Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. Jesus was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification, so the righteousness credited to believers is grounded in His death and resurrection. Abraham believed the promise; we believe the promise kept in Christ.
basic.god: God is the one who justifies the ungodly, who credits righteousness apart from works, who gives life to the dead and calls into being what does not exist. He is a promising God who keeps His word across the centuries, and a gracious God who accepts sinners on the basis of faith so that salvation rests entirely on His grace and not on human achievement.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15:6 is the seed text: Abraham believed the LORD, and it was counted to him as righteousness. Psalm 32:1-2 adds David's testimony to the blessedness of forgiven sin. Galatians 3 makes the same argument, that those of faith are Abraham's children. James 2 speaks of faith proven by works, a complement not a contradiction.
basic.oneStory: God promised Abraham a family as numerous as the stars, and Abraham simply believed, and was counted righteous. The rest of the Bible unfolds that promise until it opens to all nations in Christ. The God who raised life from a barren womb finally raised Jesus from the grave, and everyone who trusts Him is counted righteous and welcomed into Abraham's worldwide family.

context.literary: Romans 4 is a single, tightly argued exposition of Genesis 15:6, structured around the repeated word credited. Paul moves through Abraham's justification by faith not works, the timing before circumcision, the promise through faith not law, and a climactic description of Abraham's faith, before applying it directly to believers in verses 23 to 25. Psalm 32 is woven in as a second witness. The chapter is the scriptural proof of the thesis stated in Romans 3:21-31.
context.historical: For a mixed church of Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome, the status of Abraham was decisive. Jewish tradition often held Abraham up as the great example of law-keeping and merit, even crediting him with keeping the law before it was given. Paul confronts that reading head on, insisting that Abraham was justified by faith before circumcision, so that Gentile believers stand on exactly the same footing as Jewish ones.
context.canonical: This chapter, with Genesis 15:6 and Galatians 3, forms the biblical backbone of the doctrine of justification by faith. It shows the Old and New Testaments teaching one gospel: sinners are counted righteous through faith. The imputation, or crediting, of righteousness became a central pillar of the Reformation's recovery of the gospel of grace.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prove from Scripture that justification is by faith and not by works, using Abraham and David as witnesses, so that both Jewish and Gentile believers understand they are accepted on the same ground, and to lead them to the same faith that trusts the God who raised Jesus.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative of Abraham describes how he was justified, but Paul explicitly makes it prescriptive: the words it was credited to him were written also for us. The way Abraham was counted righteous is the abiding pattern for how every believer is justified.

debates.question: Does Paul's teaching on justification by faith contradict James, who says faith without works is dead?

views.label: They are complementary
views.case: The historic reading holds that Paul speaks of the ground of justification before God, which is faith alone apart from works, while James speaks of the evidence of living faith, which shows itself in works. Both cite Genesis 15:6; the faith that justifies is never alone but always bears fruit.

views.label: They stand in real tension
views.case: Some argue Paul and James use justify and works in genuinely different senses that resist harmony, reflecting distinct emphases in the early church.

debates.bearing: The historic reading is foregrounded: we are justified by faith alone, but the faith that justifies is a living faith that works. Paul guards the freeness of grace; James guards against a dead, barren profession.

debates.question: What does it mean that righteousness was credited to Abraham?

views.label: A righteousness reckoned from outside
views.case: The Reformation reading takes credited as imputation: God counts to the believer a righteousness that is not his own achievement, grounded in Christ, received by faith. The accounting language and the contrast with wages support this.

views.label: Faith itself counted as the righteous act
views.case: Some read it as God graciously accepting the act of believing in the place of righteousness, so that faith is what is reckoned as righteous.

debates.bearing: Either way justification is by grace through faith and not by works; the historic reading rightly stresses that the righteousness is a gift credited to the ungodly, so that boasting is excluded and Christ's finished work is the ground.

study.originalLanguages: The engine of the chapter is logizomai, to reckon or credit, an accounting term Paul repeats over and over: God enters righteousness on the account of the one who believes. What is credited is dikaiosyne, a right standing before God, and it is given, not earned, for the worker receives a wage as an obligation while the believer receives righteousness as a gift. That gift is received by pistis, faith, the trust that does not work but relies on Him who justifies the ungodly. And the shape of that faith is elpis, hope against hope, the confidence that the God who gives life to the dead will do exactly what He promised.

typology.type: Abraham the father of many nations
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as Gentiles who share his faith are brought into his family, so that he fathers a worldwide people of faith in Christ, Genesis 17:5 and Galatians 3:7-9.

typology.type: Life from a dead body and a barren womb
typology.fulfillment: Points forward to the resurrection: the God who gave Abraham a son against nature is the God who raised Jesus from the dead.
The pattern of justification by faith, fulfilled and made explicit in the crediting of Christ's righteousness to all who believe.

study.christ: Though Abraham fills the chapter, it lands on Christ. The faith that justifies now rests on Him who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead. Verse 25 gives the gospel in miniature: Jesus was delivered over to death for our trespasses and raised to life for our justification. His death answers our sin and His resurrection secures our acquittal, so the righteousness credited to believers is anchored in His cross and empty tomb. Abraham trusted the promise; we trust the Promised One who has come, died, and risen.
study.god: This chapter presents a God who justifies the ungodly, a startling phrase, for He does not wait until sinners make themselves worthy but credits righteousness to those who simply believe. He is the God who gives life to the dead and calls into being what does not exist, the God who keeps promises across centuries. Because acceptance rests on His grace received by faith, all boasting is silenced and all glory returns to Him.

crossReferences.note: Abraham believed the LORD, and it was counted to him as righteousness, the text Paul expounds throughout the chapter.
David's blessing on the one whose sin the Lord does not count against him, a second witness to righteousness apart from works.
Those who have faith are children of Abraham, justified by faith like him and blessed in Christ.
The promise that Abraham would be a father of many nations, fulfilled as believing Gentiles join his family.

study.oneStory: God called Abraham and promised him offspring beyond counting, and Abraham believed, and was reckoned righteous. That promise ran through Israel and finally burst its banks to reach every nation in Christ. The God who brought a son from a dead body and a barren womb is the same God who raised Jesus from the grave. Now all who believe in Him, delivered for our sins and raised for our justification, are counted righteous and gathered into Abraham's countless family.

quiz.q: According to verse 3, how was Abraham counted righteous?

options.text: He believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness.
options.note: Paul quotes Genesis 15:6: righteousness came by faith, not works.

options.text: By a mixture of faith and his obedience to God's commands.
options.note: Faith is the way, but Paul excludes works entirely; the worker earns a wage, while Abraham simply believed.

options.text: By his works, which gave him something to boast about before God.
options.note: Verse 2 denies this: even if justified by works he could not boast before God.

options.text: By being circumcised as a sign of the covenant.
options.note: The crediting came before circumcision, as verses 9 to 11 show.

quiz.q: Why does Paul stress that Abraham was justified before he was circumcised?

options.text: So that he is the father of all who believe, both uncircumcised and circumcised.
options.note: The timing makes Abraham father of believing Gentiles and Jews alike.

options.text: To show that circumcision no longer had any meaning for Israel.
options.note: Circumcision was a seal of righteousness already had by faith, not meaningless, but it was never the ground.

options.text: To prove Abraham was actually a Gentile.
options.note: Abraham was the forefather of Israel; the point is the order of faith before circumcision.

options.text: Because circumcision came only later under Moses.
options.note: Circumcision was given to Abraham himself; Paul's point is that faith preceded it.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the faith of Abraham in verses 18 to 21?

options.text: Against all hope he believed in hope, fully persuaded God could do what He promised.
options.note: His faith trusted the life-giving God despite his and Sarah's deadness.

options.text: A faith that never faced any doubt or obstacle at all.
options.note: He faced the deadness of his body and Sarah's womb, yet did not waver in unbelief.

options.text: A faith based on visible evidence that a son was already coming.
options.note: There was no natural evidence; he trusted the promise, not sight.

options.text: A confidence in his own strength to father a child.
options.note: He acknowledged his body was as good as dead and trusted God, not himself.

quiz.q: What does verse 25 say about the death and resurrection of Jesus?

options.text: He was delivered over for our trespasses and raised for our justification.
options.note: His death answers our sin and His resurrection secures our acquittal.

options.text: He died as an example of faith for us to imitate.
options.note: His death does move us, but it was for our trespasses, a substitution, not merely an example.

options.text: He was raised so that we could follow Abraham's works.
options.note: He was raised for our justification, grounding righteousness by faith, not works.

options.text: His death and resurrection matter mainly for Israel.
options.note: Verse 24 says it is credited to all of us who believe, Jew and Gentile.

quiz.q: What is the difference Paul draws between the worker and the believer in verses 4 to 5?

options.text: The worker is paid a wage as an obligation, but the believer's faith is credited as a gift.
options.note: Grace and wages are opposites; righteousness is credited, not earned.

options.text: Both receive the same reward, whether they work or believe.
options.note: Both do receive something, but Paul contrasts them: a wage is owed, while righteousness is a free gift.

options.text: The believer must first work and then also believe.
options.note: Paul speaks of the one who does not work but believes in Him who justifies the ungodly.

options.text: The worker earns a righteousness superior to the believer's.
options.note: No one is justified by works; the believer receives righteousness by grace.

questions: Abraham was justified not by working but by believing Him who justifies the ungodly. How does it change your standing before God to know that righteousness is credited as a gift and not earned as a wage?
Paul says the words it was credited to him were written for us too. How does reading Abraham's story as your own story strengthen your assurance today?
Against all hope Abraham believed in hope, fully persuaded God could keep His promise. Where are you tempted to weigh the deadness of your circumstances rather than the power of the promising God?
Abraham gave glory to God as he grew strong in faith. How is trusting God's promise itself a way of honoring Him, even before you see the answer?
Jesus was delivered for our trespasses and raised for our justification. How does resting on His finished work, rather than your performance, free you to face your failures this week?

translationNotes.note: Credited renders logizomai, an accounting term meaning to reckon or put to one's account; it recurs eleven times in the chapter and is the key to Paul's argument that righteousness is entered on the believer's account as a gift.

The BSB uses an em dash after offspring; here it is rendered with a comma to avoid the dash while preserving the sense that the promise is guaranteed to all Abraham's offspring, both those of the law and those of his faith.
The BSB uses an em dash before for us who believe; here it is rendered with a comma. The point is that the crediting of righteousness applies now to all who trust the God who raised Jesus.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 4:1-25. Footnote markers were skipped and poem lines in verses 7 and 8 joined with spaces. The em dashes in verses 16 and 24 were rendered as commas to avoid dashes while preserving the sense. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
